source.title: Benzodiazepine use and risk of Alzheimer's disease: case-control study

confidence_rationale: Case-control design with pharmacy database (Quebec RAMQ), good sample size (1796 cases + 7184 controls), 5-year exposure lag, dose-response relationship (OR 1.84 at >180 prescribed daily doses), persistence of association after adjustment for anxiety/depression/insomnia. Longer-acting benzodiazepines had higher OR (1.70 vs 1.43 for short-acting) -- consistent with cumulative SWS+REM suppression hypothesis. Transfer risk is moderate: causal direction is uncertain (reverse causation remains possible despite the exposure lag), and the mechanistic pathway through sleep phase disruption is not tested.
failure_signatures: Reverse causation is a real concern even with the 5-year exposure lag: anxiety and insomnia (early prodromal dementia features) may drive benzodiazepine prescribing before diagnosis, though adjustment for these comorbidities did not substantially change the OR.
The study cannot distinguish whether SWS suppression, REM suppression, or direct GABAergic neurotoxicity is the causal mechanism -- MECH-174 predicts SWS+REM pathway but the design cannot test this.

Long-acting vs short-acting differential (OR 1.70 vs 1.43) is consistent with cumulative pipeline suppression but is equally consistent with differential half-life producing more residual daytime sedation and encoding impairment.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Benzodiazepine use started at least 5 years before diagnosis is associated with increased Alzheimer's disease risk; adjusted OR 1.51 (95% CI 1.36-1.69); dose-response clear (OR 1.84 at >180 prescribed daily doses); long-acting drugs have higher OR (1.70) than short-acting (1.43). Adjustment for anxiety, depression, and insomnia as potential confounders/prodromal markers did not substantially alter the association.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-174 predicts benzodiazepines impair Phases 1 and 2 of the attribution pipeline via SWS homeostasis suppression (MECH-120) and SWR replay fragmentation (MECH-121). INV-048 predicts this pharmacological mechanism is equivalent to behavioral sleep deprivation in producing pipeline degradation. The dose-response and long-acting drug differential are consistent with cumulative pipeline damage accruing over time.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The association supports MECH-174 and INV-048 but cannot rule out alternative mechanisms: direct GABAergic neurodegeneration (GABA-A receptor downregulation in hippocampus), daytime sedation impairing encoding (distinct from pipeline disruption during sleep), or residual confounding by indication. MECH-174 predicts a specific PSG signature (reduced SWS depth and continuity) that this epidemiological design cannot test.
mapping.source_context: Case-control, Quebec RAMQ insurance database; adults 66+; 1796 cases, 7184 matched controls; computerised dispensing records; Alzheimer's diagnosis from provincial health records.

confidence_components.notes: Moderate mapping fidelity: the epidemiological association is strong but mechanism cannot be tested with this design. Transfer risk elevated due to causal uncertainty.
